Plywood / melamine blade (80+ teeth): Ultra-clean cuts in veneered plywood and melamine without chip-out.
Combination blade (40-50 teeth): A do-everything blade that crosscuts and rips reasonably well. Best single blade if you only want one.
secondary_uses: Metal-cutting blade: Cuts metal studs, thin steel, aluminum
Cement board blade: Polycrystalline diamond or carbide blade for HardieBacker and cement board
Diamond blade: Cuts tile, stone, and masonry on an angle grinder or tile saw
NOT_for: Cutting materials the blade isn't designed for (a wood blade on metal will ruin the blade and could be dangerous)
Using a dull blade and compensating with force (change the blade — dull blades are more dangerous than sharp ones)

overlaps_with.overlap: Miter saws and circular saws use the same diameter blades (7-1/4 inch or 10 inch). You can swap blades between them if the arbor hole matches.

can_replace: nothing — this IS the cutting element. The saw is just the motor that spins it.
replaced_by: different blade type when cutting different materials
tools.rent_vs_buy: Buy — blades are consumable items. Get a 24-tooth framing blade for rough work and a 60-tooth finish blade for clean cuts. Combination (40T) blade is the best single blade to start with.
tools.buy_cordless_reason: This is an accessory, not a powered tool.

tools.what_it_does: A tabletop saw with a diamond blade that cuts tile, stone, and porcelain while spraying water to keep the blade cool and suppress dust. It makes clean, precise cuts in ceramic, porcelain, marble, granite, and glass tile that no other tool can match.
primary_uses: Cutting ceramic and porcelain floor tile to size
Cutting wall tile for kitchen and bathroom
Making L-shaped and notch cuts around outlets and fixtures
Cutting natural stone (marble, granite, travertine)
Cutting glass mosaic tile
secondary_uses: Cutting thin brick veneer
Cutting cement pavers
Miter-cutting tile edges for corner joints (on saws with tilt capability)
NOT_for: Wood, metal, or anything that isn't tile/stone/masonry
Quick single cuts (setup and cleanup time make it overkill for one cut — use a manual tile cutter or angle grinder with a diamond blade for a few cuts)
Cutting tile on site without water access (the water system is essential)

overlaps_with.overlap: An angle grinder with a diamond blade can cut tile, but the cuts are rougher, dustier, and less controlled. For a few cuts or rough cuts, an angle grinder works. For a full tile job (bathroom floor, shower, backsplash), you need a wet tile saw.
A manual tile cutter (score and snap) handles straight cuts in ceramic tile quickly and cheaply. But it can't make L-cuts, notches, or cut porcelain/stone. A wet saw handles everything.

can_replace: angle grinder with diamond blade for tile cutting (much cleaner and more precise)
manual tile cutter for complex cuts
replaced_by: angle grinder with diamond blade for a few quick cuts (rental cost not worth it for 3 cuts)
tools.rent_vs_buy: Rent for most homeowners. A decent wet saw rents for $50-75/day. You'll only tile a bathroom once every 10-20 years. Buy ($100-200 for a basic 7-inch model) only if you plan to tile multiple rooms or enjoy tile work.
tools.buy_cordless_reason: Wet tile saws need a continuous water supply and consistent power. All models are corded. This is a shop/jobsite tool, not a portable one.

tools.what_it_does: Holds pieces of wood, metal, or other materials together while glue dries, while you drill, while you cut, or while you assemble something. Woodworkers say you can never have too many clamps. They're the extra pair of hands you don't have.
primary_uses: Holding glued pieces together while the glue dries
Clamping a straight-edge guide to sheet goods for circular saw cuts
Holding workpieces to a bench while you drill, sand, or cut
Assembling cabinets, shelves, and furniture
Securing a speed square or fence to guide a tool
secondary_uses: Temporarily holding boards in position during framing
Clamping a stop block to a miter saw fence for repeated cuts
Holding material for welding
Improvised vise for small workpieces
NOT_for: Permanent fastening (clamps are temporary — use screws, nails, or glue for permanent connections)
Heavy structural loads (use bolts and brackets)

overlaps_with.overlap: A vise mounted to a bench holds workpieces for filing, sawing, and drilling. Clamps are more portable and versatile, but a vise is more secure for aggressive work.

can_replace: weights and heavy objects for clamping pressure
replaced_by: nothing — clamps are irreplaceable in woodworking
tools.rent_vs_buy: Buy — start with 4-6 quick-grip bar clamps (6-inch and 12-inch). Add more as needed. Clamps are cheap ($5-15 each for basic trigger clamps) and you'll use them constantly.
tools.buy_cordless_reason: Clamps are manual tools. No power needed.

tools.what_it_does: A portable, bright LED light that illuminates work areas where existing lighting is insufficient. Essential for renovation work (the power may be off), basement finishing, attic work, and any job site before the permanent lighting is installed.
primary_uses: Illuminating work areas during renovation (when overhead lights are removed or power is off)
Lighting up basements, attics, crawl spaces, and garages
Providing task lighting for detail work (tiling, trim installation, painting)
Lighting outdoor work areas for early morning or evening projects
secondary_uses: Emergency lighting during power outages
Inspection lighting for plumbing and electrical work inside walls
Photography and video lighting for documentation
Camping and outdoor activities
NOT_for: Permanent lighting installation (use hardwired fixtures)

overlaps_with.overlap: A headlamp keeps your hands free and lights wherever you look — great for working inside walls, under sinks, and in crawl spaces. A work light illuminates a larger area. Both are useful.

replaced_by: permanent installed lighting once a project is complete
tools.rent_vs_buy: Buy — LED work lights start at $20 and are incredibly useful. Get a battery-powered one that matches your tool battery ecosystem for convenience.
tools.buy_cordless_reason: Battery-powered work lights are essential — you're often working in spaces where the power is disconnected or outlets don't exist yet. Most tool brands offer lights that use the same batteries as their power tools.

tools.what_it_does: A retractable string coated in chalk dust. Stretch it between two points, pull it up, and snap it against a surface to mark a perfectly straight line over long distances. Essential for layout work — framing walls, installing flooring, setting tile, and marking cut lines on plywood.
primary_uses: Marking straight layout lines on subfloors for framing walls
Marking guidelines for installing first row of flooring, tile, or drywall
Marking long straight cut lines on plywood and sheet goods
Marking stud locations on plates for wall framing
Marking reference lines for installing siding
secondary_uses: Marking lines on concrete for forms or layout
Creating grid lines for tile layout
Marking rip lines on long boards
NOT_for: Short measurements (use a pencil and square)
Permanent marks (chalk wipes off — use a marker for permanent lines)
Precision under 1/16 inch (the chalk line has some thickness)

overlaps_with.overlap: A laser level projects a visible line on a wall for trim and cabinet installation. A chalk line marks a line on a floor or large surface. They're complementary — laser for walls, chalk for floors and sheet goods.

can_replace: straight edge and pencil for long lines
replaced_by: laser level for wall layout
tools.rent_vs_buy: Buy — costs $5-12. It's a consumable item (refill the chalk) that you'll use on any construction or flooring project.
tools.buy_cordless_reason: Manual tool — no power needed.

tools.what_it_does: A long, straight aluminum or steel rail that clamps to material and guides your circular saw for straight cuts. It turns a $100 circular saw into a tool that makes cuts nearly as straight as a $400 track saw. The poor man's track saw.
primary_uses: Making straight rip cuts in plywood and sheet goods with a circular saw
Breaking down full sheets of plywood into manageable sizes
Ripping boards to consistent width
Making long straight crosscuts on wide boards
secondary_uses: Guiding a router along a straight line for dadoes and grooves
Guiding a jigsaw for straighter cuts
NOT_for: Curved cuts (no guide helps with curves — use a jigsaw)
Short crosscuts on narrow boards (use a speed square instead)
Replacing a track saw for ultra-precision work (a track saw with its anti-splinter strip is still cleaner)

overlaps_with.overlap: A straight-edge guide + circular saw is 80% as good as a track saw at 20% of the price. The track saw adds anti-splinter strips, plunge capability, and slightly better precision. For a budget-conscious DIYer, the guide + circular saw is the way to start.
Both make straight rip cuts. A table saw is faster for repeated cuts, but a guide + circular saw works for occasional ripping without a stationary tool.

can_replace: track saw for budget-conscious straight cuts
table saw for occasional sheet good ripping
replaced_by: track-saw for premium precision
table-saw for production ripping
tools.rent_vs_buy: Buy — $20-50 for a basic straight edge, $40-80 for a Kreg Rip-Cut or Bora WTX clamp edge. Essential accessory if you own a circular saw and work with plywood.
tools.buy_cordless_reason: It's a passive guide — no power needed.

beginner_buying_order.description: If you're starting from zero tools, buy them roughly in this order. The first 5 will handle 80% of home projects.

tiers.name: The Absolute Essentials
tiers.note: Buy the drill and impact as a combo kit — much better value than separate. Add the three hand tools for under $50 total. This gets you through hanging shelves, assembling furniture, basic repairs, and 80% of home tasks.

tiers.name: Ready for Real Projects
tiers.note: Now you can cut wood, sand surfaces, make plunge cuts, and find studs. You can tackle a deck, build raised garden beds, refinish furniture, and do basic renovations.

tiers.name: Renovation Ready
tiers.note: Install trim, make curved cuts, clean up properly, and work in dark spaces. You can now handle a bathroom renovation, install flooring, finish a basement, and build a shed.

tiers.name: Serious DIYer
tiers.note: Full workshop capability. You can build cabinets, do precision woodworking, handle demolition, cut tile and metal, and drill into concrete. At this point, you can tackle nearly any home project.
